ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for custard bun; 'bread product (food)' is the closest defensible parent class. A more specific filled-bread or dim-sum term is not available in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated custard bun code; A000E (Bread and similar products) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for custard bun as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard custard bun weighs approximately 80 g, providing around 226 kcal, 31 g carbohydrate, 7 g protein, and 7 g fat per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Custard Bun — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all per_100g fields. Values are accepted as-is. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, potassium, and micronutrient detail not reported by HPB for this entry; set to -1 where not available.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for custard bun in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 71 is estimated from white bread / enriched white flour bun analogues (GI ~70–72) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and the University of Sydney GI Database. The custard filling adds sugar but does not substantially alter the GI of the bread matrix. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × available carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 71 × 31.2 / 100 ≈ 22.2, rounded to 22.1 using 80 g serving size (39.2 g carb/100g × 0.80 = 31.36 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar content (10.3 g/100g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; sodium is moderate at 159 mg/100g
hypertension.guidance: Limit to one small bun per sitting; the 10.3 g/100g sugar load promotes insulin-driven renal sodium retention, which can elevate blood pressure independently of dietary sodium, and should be paired with a high-fibre food to blunt the glycaemic spike.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic index (~71) and high carbohydrate load (39.2 g/100g) with only 2.3 g/100g dietary fibre and 10.3 g/100g sugar from custard filling
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit custard bun to half a bun per occasion, pair it with a protein source such as a boiled egg or tofu to slow glucose absorption, and prefer whole-wheat or reduced-sugar custard variants to lower the glycaemic load from the current estimated GL of ~22 per standard serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Refined flour base, added sugar (10.3 g/100g), and moderate fat (8.6 g/100g) with unknown saturated fat fraction from egg yolk and butter in custard
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should treat custard bun as an occasional food; the refined carbohydrate and sugar content raise triglyceride risk, and the egg-yolk custard filling contributes saturated fat and dietary cholesterol that may adversely affect LDL cholesterol when consumed regularly.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Custard bun (奶黄包 / Nai Huang Bao) is a soft, lightly sweetened bread roll filled with a smooth, creamy egg-custard filling made from egg yolks, sugar, butter, milk, and custard powder. It is widely sold at bakeries and dim-sum establishments across Singapore, Malaysia, and the broader Southeast Asian region. The bun may be baked — producing a golden, slightly glossy crust — or steamed, yielding a pillowy white exterior. The custard filling is warm, rich, and mildly sweet with a silky texture. Per 100 g, the bun provides 283 kcal, 9.3 g protein, 8.6 g fat, 39.2 g carbohydrate, 10.3 g sugar, 2.3 g dietary fibre, 159 mg sodium, 73 mg calcium, and 1.3 mg iron, based on the Singapore HPB Foodo database. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 71 (high), producing a glycaemic load of roughly 22 per standard 80 g serving. Clinically, the high refined-carbohydrate and sugar content makes this food unsuitable for frequent consumption by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. It is best consumed occasionally and paired with protein or fibre-rich foods to moderate the glycaemic response. Common meal contexts include breakfast and mid-morning snack.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a custard bun?
Is custard bun healthy for diabetics?
Can I eat custard bun if I have high blood pressure?
Custard bun vs red bean bun nutrition

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the Singapore HPB Foodo database at confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using white-flour bun equivalents, which is the closest approved GI source available. Multilingual names are well-established for this food category.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB for this entry): saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Reason: the HPB Foodo database entry for custard bun does not report detailed fatty acid fractions, cholesterol, potassium, or micronutrient detail beyond calcium and iron. No alternative approved source was consulted for these fields as the HPB record is the canonical source for this Singaporean food. GI of 71 is an analogous estimate based on white bread / enriched white flour bun data from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for custard bun was found in any approved source. Glycaemic load per 80 g serving ≈ 22.1. The input health_suitability entries for 'diabetes' and 'rheumatoid_arthritis' have been mapped to 'type2_diabetes' and 'rheumatoid_arthritis' respectively in additional_conditions context; the three mandatory conditions (hypertension, type2_diabetes, cardiovascular_disease) are fully populated. Custard bun is a Singaporean/Malaysian bakery staple; multilingual names provided in English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per G6 mandatory language rules for Singaporean dishes.
